era: Rare convergence event. Typically late autumn or early spring during territorial or breeding periods. The rarest acoustic convergence in the Pacific Northwest ecosystem.
ecosystem: Remote wilderness (North Cascades / Selkirks / Idaho border wilderness)
season: Rare convergence (late autumn or early spring, territorial / breeding period)
species: Puma concolor (cougar / mountain lion / puma) -- Felidae, apex predator, widespread but rarely heard
Canis lupus (gray wolf) -- Canidae, apex predator, recolonized Washington State 2008
Ursus arctos horribilis (grizzly bear) -- Ursidae, apex predator, small recovering population Selkirks/North Cascades

theory_nodes.concept: Cougar vocalization: the scream. Puma concolor does not roar like a lion; it screams. The cougar's primary vocalization is a high-pitched, piercing wail that carries for miles in wilderness conditions. Often described as sounding like a human woman screaming -- but sustained longer, at higher peak amplitude, with a quality of absolute wildness that human screaming does not contain. Frequency range: 500-4,000 Hz, with peak amplitude around 1,000-2,000 Hz. In total darkness in remote wilderness, a cougar scream is one of the most physiologically alarming sounds a human can encounter. The body responds before the mind categorizes.
theory_nodes.description: Cougar scream -- high-pitched, piercing, physiologically alarming in the dark.

theory_nodes.concept: Gray wolf pack howl: the social architecture of wilderness. Wolf howls function simultaneously as territorial advertisement, pack-cohesion mechanism, and long-distance communication. A full pack howl -- multiple adults at different pitches, harmonizing and counter-harmonizing -- creates a sound that is both singular (clearly wolf) and complex (the pack as a single acoustic entity with multiple voices). Frequency range: 150-1,500 Hz, with harmonics extending to 5,000 Hz. Duration: 3-7 seconds per individual howl, with overlapping howls creating a sustained acoustic event. The howl carries 6-10 miles in wilderness conditions.
theory_nodes.description: Gray wolf pack howl -- social architecture made audible, 6-10 mile range.

theory_nodes.concept: Grizzly bear vocalization: the low-frequency warning. Grizzly bears (Ursus arctos horribilis) vocalize during territorial encounters, threat displays, and mating season using a combination of low-frequency grunts, huffs, and the full-volume vocalization: the roar. Grizzly roar frequency range: 80-500 Hz, with peak amplitude in the 100-200 Hz range. The roar is not sustained like a wolf howl or sharp like a cougar scream; it is a concussive burst of low-frequency energy that registers as physical impact at close range. The Washington State grizzly population (Selkirks, recovering North Cascades) represents the southernmost range of the species in the contiguous US.
theory_nodes.description: Grizzly bear roar -- low-frequency concussive burst, 80-500 Hz.

theory_nodes.concept: The convergence probability: the probability of cougar, wolf pack, and grizzly bear all vocalizing in the same wilderness acoustic space on the same night is extraordinarily low. Each species actively avoids the others in most circumstances (inter-predator competition and avoidance is well-documented). The convergence occurs most plausibly when: (1) territorial pressure forces multiple species into the same area simultaneously, (2) a prey concentration draws multiple predators to the same zone, or (3) breeding season activity in overlapping territories generates simultaneous nocturnal vocalizations without direct interaction. When it occurs, it is the acoustic maximum of the PNW terrestrial ecosystem: three species at the apex of the food web, vocalizing simultaneously, in the same wilderness space.
theory_nodes.description: The convergence probability -- why this event is the rarest acoustic convergence in the PNW ecosystem.

theory_nodes.concept: The frequency tapestry: the three voices create a full-spectrum acoustic event when simultaneous. Wolf howl (150-1,500 Hz, sustained, harmonically complex) occupies the midrange and extends into lower treble. Grizzly roar (80-500 Hz, concussive burst) occupies the low midrange and upper bass. Cougar scream (500-4,000 Hz, piercing, sustained) occupies the upper midrange and lower treble. Together: wolf + grizzly + cougar covers 80-4,000 Hz simultaneously, with the three voices distinguishable but overlapping in the 150-1,500 Hz zone where all three are active. A full-spectrum acoustic portrait of the apex predator triad.
theory_nodes.description: The frequency tapestry -- three voices creating full-spectrum apex predator acoustic event.

theory_nodes.concept: Washington State ecology context: gray wolves recolonized Washington State from Idaho beginning around 2008; current population approximately 200+ individuals in multiple packs. Grizzly bears: a small population persists in the Selkirk Mountains (northeast Washington) and a recovering population is documented in the North Cascades. Cougars: stable population statewide, Washington Department of Fish and Wildlife estimates 1,500-2,000 adults. All three species are present in Washington; the convergence is geographically plausible in the remote wilderness of the Selkirks and North Cascades.
theory_nodes.description: Washington State apex predator ecology -- all three species present and their current status.

cross_references.relationship: E=10 + E=10 = E=20. Summit's third E=20. Triple apex predator convergence (rarest acoustic event, three species at maximum simultaneous intensity) paired with the most emotionally intense album in the grunge tradition. Both: multiple powerful forces in the same space, simultaneously, at maximum intensity.

Both E=10. The Cascades Full Storm is the physical-meteorological maximum; the triple apex predator convergence is the biological maximum. Both occupy the same wilderness space (Cascade Range) at different levels of the ecological hierarchy.

Both E=10. The triple apex predator convergence is the terrestrial acoustic maximum; the full SRKW greeting is the marine acoustic maximum. The summit's final two SPS entries document the opposite ecological domains at the same energy level.

sps_pair.pairing_note: E=10 + E=10 = E=20. Summit's third E=20. Both document the simultaneous presence of multiple powerful forces at maximum intensity in the same space -- one in the wilderness at night, one in a recording studio in 1992.

milestones: FULL SPECTRUM 80-4000 HZ APEX ACOUSTIC EVENT
convergence_ecology: Gray wolf recolonized Washington 2008, ~200+ individuals. Grizzly bear: Selkirks (confirmed) + North Cascades (recovering). Cougar: ~1,500-2,000 adults statewide. All three present in remote northeast Washington and North Cascades wilderness.
